Death of Nectarius and Ordination of John.

ccel.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

title: Death of Nectarius and Ordination of John.

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: A short time after Nectarius also, bishop of Constantinople died, during the consulate of Cæsarius and Atticus, on the 27th of September.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: A contest thereupon immediately arose respecting the appointment of a successor, some proposing one person, and some another: at length however it was determined to send for John, a presbyter of the church at Antioch, for there was a report that he was very instructive, and at the same time eloquent.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: By the general consent therefore of both the clergy and laity, he was summoned very soon afterwards to Constantinople by the Emperor Arcadius: and to render the ordination more authoritative and imposing, several prelates were requested to be present, among whom also was Theophilus bishop of Alexandria.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This person did everything he could to detract from John’s reputation, being desirous of promoting to that see, Isidore a presbyter of his own church, to whom he was greatly attached, on account of a very delicate and perilous affair which Isidore had undertaken to serve his interests.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: What this was I must now unfold.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: While the Emperor Theodosius was preparing to attack the usurper Maximus, Theodosius sent Isidore with gifts giving twofold letters, and enjoining him to present both the gifts and the proper letters to him who should become the victor.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: In accordance with these injunctions Isidore on his arrival at Rome awaited there the event of the war.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But this business did not long remain a secret: for a reader who accompanied him privately sequestered the letters; upon which Isidore in great alarm returned to Alexandria.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This was the reason why Theophilus so warmly favored Isidore.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The court however gave the preference to John: and inasmuch as many had revived the accusations against Theophilus, and prepared for presentation to the bishops then convened memorials of various charges, Eutropius the chief officer of the imperial bed-chamber collected these documents, and showed them to Theophilus, bidding him ‘choose between ordaining John, and undergoing a trial on the charges made against him.’

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Theophilus terrified at this alternative, consented to ordain John.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Accordingly John was invested with the episcopal dignity on the 26th of February, under the following consulate, which the Emperor Honorius celebrated with public games at Rome, and Eutychian, then Prætorian prefect, at Constantinople.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But since the man is famous, both for the writings he has left, and the many troubles he fell into, it is proper that I should not pass over his affairs in silence, but to relate as compendiously as possible whence he was, and from what ancestry; also the particulars of his elevation to the episcopate, and the means by which he was subsequently degraded; and finally how he was more honored after his death, than he had been during his life.
